title: Cat Ear Discharge: Why It Looks Like Coffee Grounds
meta_desc: Find out what coffee-ground discharge in your cat's ear means, why it is usually a low-urgency issue, and how photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
What is this dark discharge?

Finding dark, crumbly material in your cat's ear that resembles coffee grounds is a classic sign of ear mites (Otodectes cynotis). These microscopic parasites live in the ear canal and feed on wax and skin oils. The dark debris you see is actually a mixture of earwax, dried blood, and mite waste. Occasionally, a yeast or bacterial infection can also produce dark discharge, though these often have a more distinct, unpleasant odor compared to mites.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for coffee-ground ear discharge is typically low. While the condition is itchy and uncomfortable for your feline friend, it is not a life-threatening emergency. You should aim to schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 48 to 72 hours. However, if you notice your cat is tilting their head significantly, stumbling, or if the ear flap itself has become hot and swollen, you should contact a vet sooner as these may indicate a more advanced infection or a hematoma.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photograph of the inside of your cat's ear can be an invaluable tool for your veterinarian or a triage assistant. A high-quality image allows professionals to see the exact texture and distribution of the debris. Because ear mites and certain fungal infections can look very similar to the naked eye, a photo helps determine if immediate intervention is needed or if the issue can wait for a scheduled appointment. It also provides a baseline to monitor if the discharge is worsening while you wait for your visit.

faqs.question: Can ear mites spread to my other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear mites are highly contagious among cats and dogs. If one pet is diagnosed, it is very likely that all other furry residents in the home will need to be treated simultaneously.

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter drops to fix this?
faqs.answer: It is best to wait for a veterinary diagnosis. If your cat has a ruptured eardrum, certain medications can cause permanent deafness. A vet must confirm the eardrum is intact before any liquid medication is applied.

faqs.question: Will ear mites go away on their own?
faqs.answer: No, ear mites will not resolve without treatment. Left untreated, they can lead to severe secondary bacterial infections and chronic inflammation of the ear canal.
